mechanism_narrative: The federal funds rate has been pinned near zero (0.16%) since the crisis, compressing the discount rate applied to future equity cash flows and mechanically supporting valuations. As long as FEDFUNDS remains near its effective lower bound, this valuation support persists and produces a persistent positive drift in S&P 500 forward returns regardless of other conditions.
failure_condition: Rank IC between low-FEDFUNDS level and forward 20d returns falls below 0.03 over any rolling 2-year window, or returns during ZIRP periods are not significantly different from full-sample average returns (t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: When UNRATE declines by more than 0.2 percentage points over a rolling 3-month window, it signals accelerating labor market healing that supports consumer income and corporate earnings expectations, producing positive S&P 500 forward returns over the subsequent month. Below this threshold the signal is inactive.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE falls by more than 0.2pp over trailing 3 months
evidence_refs: fact:UNRATE at 7.8% and declining from crisis peak as of Dec 2012
failure_condition: In periods where the UNRATE decline trigger fires, forward 20d SPX returns are positive in less than 55% of instances, or Rank IC < 0.03 over a 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Joint acceleration in retail sales (RSAFS) and housing starts (HOUST) indicates a broad-based consumer and housing demand recovery that is not detectable from either series alone, since retail sales can rise on non-durable spending while housing lags credit cycles. When both series show trailing 3-month growth simultaneously, forward 60-day S&P 500 returns are positively predicted; when only one accelerates, the signal is muted.
candidate_trigger: both RSAFS and HOUST show positive 3-month trailing growth simultaneously
evidence_refs: fact:HOUST at 861k units and RSAFS at $415.7bn reflecting continued post-crisis recovery
failure_condition: Joint-trigger periods do not outperform single-series-trigger periods in forward 60d returns by a statistically significant margin (p > 0.05), or Rank IC of the joint signal is not higher than either univariate signal.

mechanism_narrative: Industrial production (INDPRO) growth predicts positive S&P 500 forward returns during early-cycle recovery regimes because rising output signals expanding corporate revenues without yet triggering inflation or margin pressure concerns. In late-cycle regimes the same INDPRO growth instead correlates with capacity constraints and negative returns as markets price in overheating risk.
evidence_refs: fact:INDPRO at 97.5 with GDPC1 still below long-run trend indicating early-cycle slack
regime_hint: early-cycle recovery with output gap still open
failure_condition: The sign of the INDPRO-forward-return relationship does not flip when comparing early-cycle (output gap open, low CPIAUCSL growth) versus late-cycle (output gap closed) subsamples, or Rank IC in the early-cycle regime is below 0.03.

mechanism_narrative: A month-over-month acceleration in CPIAUCSL above 0.3% signals rising inflationary pressure that raises market expectations of earlier Federal Reserve tightening, causing a short-term negative reaction in S&P 500 prices over the following week as discount rate expectations reprice upward.
evidence_refs: fact:CPIAUCSL at 231.025 with low reported monthly inflation as of Nov 2012
regime_hint: low-inflation environment sensitive to upside surprises
failure_condition: Forward 5d SPX returns following a CPI acceleration trigger are not significantly negative (t-test p > 0.05), or positive returns occur in more than 55% of triggered instances.

mechanism_narrative: Sustained positive month-over-month growth in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) reflects an expanding labor market that supports aggregate income, consumption, and corporate revenue growth, producing a persistent positive drift in S&P 500 forward returns over a quarterly horizon whenever payroll growth trends positive.
evidence_refs: fact:PAYEMS at 134,021 thousand and rising steadily through 2012
regime_hint: any regime with positive payroll trend
failure_condition: Rank IC between trailing PAYEMS growth and forward 60d returns falls below 0.03 over any rolling 2-year window, or negative-return quarters coincide with positive PAYEMS growth in more than 50% of cases.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) recovery is amplified by persistently low federal funds rates (FEDFUNDS), since cheap mortgage financing directly enables construction activity to translate into broader demand and equity gains. The interaction of low FEDFUNDS with rising HOUST predicts stronger positive 60-day S&P 500 returns than either signal alone, because low rates alone do not guarantee housing-driven demand transmission, and rising HOUST without accommodative financing is less durable.
candidate_trigger: HOUST trailing 3-month growth positive while FEDFUNDS remains below 0.5%
evidence_refs: fact:HOUST at 861k units with FEDFUNDS at 0.16% as of Dec 2012
regime_hint: zero interest rate policy combined with housing recovery
failure_condition: Forward 60d returns during joint HOUST-uptrend/low-FEDFUNDS periods are not significantly higher than during HOUST uptrend periods with FEDFUNDS above 0.5%, or interaction-term Rank IC is not greater than either univariate proxy's Rank IC.

mechanism_narrative: A deceleration in industrial production growth (INDPRO month-over-month turning negative after two consecutive positive months) during an economic expansion signals an emerging mid-cycle slowdown that markets have not yet priced in, producing negative S&P 500 forward returns over the following week as growth expectations are revised down.
candidate_trigger: INDPRO month-over-month growth turns negative after two prior consecutive positive months
evidence_refs: fact:INDPRO at 97.509 as of Nov 2012 amid uneven post-crisis production growth
failure_condition: Forward 5d SPX returns following the INDPRO deceleration trigger are positive in more than 55% of instances, or the effect disappears (Rank IC < 0.03) when tested only within confirmed NBER expansion periods.
